name: Acropolis
classification_notes: H&Co classify Acropolis as a display face rooted in the engraved Greek lettering of nineteenth-century broadsides and architectural inscriptions. One of the four families in the Proteus Project, alongside Saracen, Leviathan and Ziggurat, sharing widths and weights with its siblings.
summary: A heavy display face by Jonathan Hoefler that draws on the engraved Greek lettering of nineteenth-century broadsides, part of the Proteus Project alongside Saracen, Leviathan and Ziggurat.
description: A display family by Jonathan Hoefler released in 1995 as part of the four-family Proteus Project. Acropolis takes its cues from the engraved Greek style that appeared on nineteenth-century broadsides, architectural inscriptions and theatrical bills, with crisp inline detail across three heavy weights. Built to be set large on posters, packaging and editorial mastheads, and designed to interchange freely with the Saracen, Leviathan and Ziggurat sibling families on shared widths and weights.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Acropolis as part of the Proteus Project, a coordinated system of four ultrabold display families drawn from different nineteenth-century traditions. Acropolis takes the engraved Greek line, sharing widths and weights with Saracen, Leviathan and Ziggurat so the four can be combined in a single composition.

name: Archer
classification_notes: H&Co classify Archer as a geometric slab serif in the tradition of mid-twentieth-century slabs like Memphis and Rockwell, but warmed with ball terminals and a true italic rather than a sloped roman. Originally commissioned for Martha Stewart Living.
summary: A geometric slab serif by Tobias Frere-Jones and Jonathan Hoefler, drawn for Martha Stewart Living, with ball terminals and a proper italic that warm the form for editorial and brand work.
description: A geometric slab serif by Tobias Frere-Jones and Jonathan Hoefler, originally commissioned for Martha Stewart Living and released for public license in 2008. Archer pairs the rational geometry of mid-century slabs with ball terminals and a drawn italic, giving it the friendliness of a magazine masthead rather than the engineering of an old Rockwell. Seven weights from Hairline to Bold with matching italics, plus Screensmart for UI and Office for documents. Used across magazines, packaging and identity work, including high-profile turns at Wells Fargo and on Barack Obama's 2008 campaign collateral.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Archer as a geometric slab serif designed for editorial work, drawing on the slab traditions of the early twentieth century but introducing ball terminals and true italics to give the family the warmth and personality more often found in serif text faces.

name: Champion Gothic
classification_notes: H&Co classify Champion Gothic as a family of six sans serifs at different widths and weights, modelled on nineteenth-century American wood type. Originally commissioned by Sports Illustrated, with each cut named after a boxing weight class.
summary: A six-cut wood-type gothic by Jonathan Hoefler, originally drawn for Sports Illustrated and named after boxing weight classes, built for sports and editorial headlines.
description: A wood-type gothic by Jonathan Hoefler, commissioned by Sports Illustrated in 1990 and built from a survey of nineteenth-century American wood type. Six cuts named for boxing weight classes, Lightweight, Welterweight, Middleweight, Featherweight, Bantamweight and Heavyweight, run from light condensed to ultrabold compressed, each tuned for headlines rather than text. The family is most familiar from American sports magazines and broadcast graphics, where its blocky, slightly irregular contours read as muscular and old-American.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Champion Gothic as a family of six sans serifs at different widths and weights, drawn from a survey of nineteenth-century American wood type and originally commissioned by Sports Illustrated. Each cut is named after a boxing weight class to signal where it sits in the family.

name: Chronicle Display
classification_notes: H&Co classify Chronicle Display as the display companion to Chronicle Text, a transitional serif in the lineage of Scotch Roman and nineteenth-century newspaper romans, drawn for the editorial scale where letters need more delicacy than a working text face.
summary: The display companion to Chronicle Text, a transitional serif system from H&Co with multiple widths and a Hairline cut for editorial headlines and decks.
description: A transitional display serif system from Hoefler & Co, conceived as the headline partner to the Chronicle Text family. Chronicle Display covers standard, Condensed and Compressed widths, with a Deck pair for sub-heads and a Hairline cut for the largest editorial settings. Five weights with matching italics give magazine and newspaper designers a coordinated kit for fronts, features and section openers, with the Scotch Roman lineage carrying the historical warmth of nineteenth-century American newspaper romans.
foundry_blurb: H&Co present Chronicle Display as part of the wider Chronicle programme, providing the display widths and a Hairline cut that sit above Chronicle Text. The family is rooted in the Scotch Roman and nineteenth-century newspaper traditions, retuned for present-day editorial publishing.

name: Chronicle Text
classification_notes: H&Co classify Chronicle Text as a workhorse newspaper and magazine text serif, drawn in four optical grades so designers can compensate for different printing conditions, papers and inks while keeping a consistent colour on the page.
summary: A graded transitional text serif from H&Co, drawn in four grades so newspaper and magazine designers can hold a consistent colour across different papers and printing conditions.
description: A transitional text serif from Hoefler & Co, designed to anchor editorial systems alongside Chronicle Display. Four grades, G1 to G4, progressively thicken the strokes so the same design can be tuned to different papers, inks and presses without changing weight on the page. Four weights with matching italics across each grade, plus Screensmart and Office cuts for screens and documents. Long body text in magazines and newspapers is the home setting, where its Scotch Roman roots read as familiar without being nostalgic.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Chronicle Text as a workhorse text serif rooted in the Scotch Roman tradition and drawn for the realities of newspaper and magazine production, with four optical grades that let designers compensate for varying print conditions while keeping the typeface visually consistent.

name: Cyclone
classification_notes: H&Co classify Cyclone as a multi-layered inline display, drawing on the high-contrast didone shaded letterforms that decorated nineteenth-century playbills, certificates and bank notes. Designed to be combined in layered colour settings.
summary: A high-contrast inline didone display from Jonathan Hoefler, built in layered cuts that revive the shaded letterforms of nineteenth-century playbills and banknotes.
description: A display family by Jonathan Hoefler that draws on the high-contrast, inline didones used on nineteenth-century playbills, currency engraving and certificates. Cyclone is built as a layered set so designers can stack colours and shadows to recreate the chromatic look of period letterpress. Intended for posters, packaging and editorial work where the typography is meant to be the ornament, not the messenger.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Cyclone as an inline display drawn from the high-contrast didone tradition of nineteenth-century playbills, banknotes and certificates, with multiple cuts that can be layered to produce chromatic, period-style effects.

name: Didot
classification_notes: H&Co classify their Didot as a multi-master didone built specifically for editorial use, with seven optical masters that adjust contrast and detail by size. Originally drawn for the relaunch of Harper's Bazaar under Fabien Baron.
summary: Jonathan Hoefler's optically-sized Didot, originally drawn for Harper's Bazaar, with seven masters tuned from caption to display.
description: An optically-sized Didot by Jonathan Hoefler, commissioned by Fabien Baron for the 1992 relaunch of Harper's Bazaar and released as a retail family afterwards. Seven masters tuned to specific sizes, from six-point text to ninety-six-point display, redistribute the contrast and hairline weight so the design holds together from caption to cover. Three weights with italics. The family is widely identified with fashion editorial and luxury branding because of the Bazaar work and the legions of mastheads that followed it.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe their Didot as a multi-master family of seven optically tuned designs, originally created for Harper's Bazaar. They emphasise that the masters address the historical problem of contrasted didones losing their hairlines at small sizes by drawing dedicated cuts for each part of the size range.

name: Forza
classification_notes: H&Co classify Forza as an industrial sans serif with squared joints and clipped terminals, drawing on the lettering of mid-twentieth-century industrial product design and signage. Sits between geometric sans and engineered display sans.
summary: An industrial sans serif by Jonathan Hoefler with squared joints and clipped corners, built for athletic and broadcast settings where the lettering needs to look engineered.
description: A sans serif by Jonathan Hoefler that recasts the geometric model in an industrial register, with squared joints, clipped terminals and a slightly mechanical posture. Six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, plus a Screensmart cut for interfaces. Forza reads as athletic and engineered, which is why it shows up across broadcast graphics, sports identities and product design, but it remains a working text-and-display sans rather than a single-purpose display face.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Forza as an industrial sans serif, drawing on mid-twentieth-century industrial design and engineering lettering. They highlight its squared joints and clipped corners as cues to the technical, engineered character they wanted the family to project.

name: Giant
classification_notes: H&Co classify Giant as a fat face didone, the heaviest end of the high-contrast didone tradition that produced posters and broadsides in early-nineteenth-century England. Drawn as a single-weight display.
summary: A fat face didone from Jonathan Hoefler that revives the heaviest end of the early-nineteenth-century English poster tradition for present-day display work.
description: A fat face display by Jonathan Hoefler, drawing on the heaviest didones of early-nineteenth-century English commercial printing. Giant pushes the contrast to the extreme, with thick verticals and fine hairlines tuned for very large sizes. Single weight with an italic, intended for posters, packaging and editorial headlines where the typography is the picture rather than the messenger. Reads as Victorian, theatrical and confidently old.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Giant as a fat face in the tradition of early-nineteenth-century English display didones, drawn for present-day large-format use and tuned to the high contrast that characterises the historical model.

name: Gotham
classification_notes: H&Co classify Gotham as a geometric sans serif rooted in the vernacular lettering of mid-twentieth-century New York, particularly the architectural signage of the Port Authority Bus Terminal. Originally commissioned by GQ magazine and now a full system of widths.
summary: Tobias Frere-Jones's geometric sans for GQ, drawn from mid-twentieth-century New York signage, now a multi-width system that has become one of the most widely used identity sans serifs of the past two decades.
description: A geometric sans serif by Tobias Frere-Jones with Jesse Ragan, originally commissioned by GQ in 2000 and built out into a full multi-width system over the following decade. Gotham takes its proportions and details from mid-twentieth-century New York vernacular lettering, especially the cast-aluminium signs of the Port Authority Bus Terminal, and softens the formal geometry of European geometric sans serifs into something distinctly American. Standard, Narrow, Extra Narrow and Condensed widths, eight weights with italics across each, plus Screensmart and Office cuts. Famously carried Barack Obama's 2008 campaign identity and the cornerstone inscription of One World Trade Center, and has spent the last two decades on more brands and books than almost any other contemporary sans.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Gotham as a geometric sans serif drawn from the vernacular signage of mid-twentieth-century New York, rather than the European geometric tradition. They highlight the family's growth into a system of widths and weights, and its widespread use in branding, publishing and public typography.

name: Gotham Rounded
classification_notes: H&Co classify Gotham Rounded as a softened sibling of Gotham, with rounded terminals across the same proportions and weights. Aimed at packaging, signage and identities where the harder edge of Gotham feels too austere.
summary: A rounded sibling of Gotham from Tobias Frere-Jones, softening the geometric sans for packaging, signage and friendlier identities.
description: A rounded companion to Gotham by Tobias Frere-Jones, sharing the parent family's proportions and weights but trading the squared terminals for cleanly rounded ends. Five weights from Light to Black with matching italics, plus a Screensmart cut for interface use. Gotham Rounded sits where the original Gotham feels too formal: kids' brands, consumer packaging, sports leisurewear, friendlier corporate identities. It keeps the New York vernacular bones of Gotham while reading warmer and more approachable.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Gotham Rounded as a softened version of Gotham, sharing the family's proportions but rounding the terminals to give the typeface a friendlier, less formal voice for branding, packaging and signage.

name: Historical Allsorts
classification_notes: H&Co package Historical Allsorts as a collection of four self-contained historical revival faces: a blackletter (St Augustin Civilite), an early roman (Great Primer Uncials), an eighteenth-century script (English Textura) and a fraktur. Sold together as a curiosity set rather than as a coordinated system.
summary: A four-face historical revival set from Jonathan Hoefler, gathering blackletter, uncial, civilite and fraktur into a single curiosity package for period-correct display work.
description: A small set of historical display revivals by Jonathan Hoefler, gathered into a single release. Historical Allsorts pulls together four self-contained faces drawn from different periods of pre-industrial typography: a civilite cursive, an early uncial roman, an English textura blackletter and a fraktur. None is meant as a working text face. They sit in editorial illustration, packaging and book design where designers want a historically literate counterpoint to a modern serif or sans without commissioning custom lettering.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Historical Allsorts as a package of four historical typefaces, each rooted in a different pre-industrial European typographic tradition. They present the collection as a resource for designers who need period-appropriate display lettering rather than as a coordinated type system.

name: Hoefler Text
classification_notes: An old-style serif commissioned by Apple as a demonstration of advanced typography on the Mac, drawing on Garamond, Janson and Caslon for a versatile reading face.
summary: Jonathan Hoefler's old-style book face, originally bundled with the Mac and built for long reading with a full kit of swashes, small caps and ornaments.
description: An old-style serif by Jonathan Hoefler that began life as a demonstration of advanced typography for Apple in 1991. It draws on Garamond, Janson and Caslon to make a workhorse reading face with proper italics, small caps, swashes, fractions and ornament sets. Sits in literary, editorial and brand settings where a quietly historical voice is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co list Hoefler Text as a versatile book face originally commissioned by Apple, drawing on the great old-style serifs to demonstrate advanced typography on the Macintosh, and equipped with a full range of swashes, small caps and ornaments.

name: Hoefler Titling
classification_notes: A display companion to Hoefler Text, drawn at large sizes for titling and refined for headlines and book jackets.
summary: The display cut of Hoefler Text, refined for titling on book jackets and editorial headlines.
description: A titling serif by Jonathan Hoefler that pairs with Hoefler Text but is drawn for large sizes. Finer hairlines, sharper serifs and tighter spacing make it suited to book jackets, magazine titles and brand wordmarks where the text family would feel too soft. Limited to a small set of weights and italics in keeping with its display role.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co describe Hoefler Titling as a display companion to Hoefler Text, drawn for use at large sizes on book jackets and editorial headlines, with sharper detail than the text family allows.

name: Ideal Sans
designer: Jonathan Hoefler, Tobias Frere-Jones, and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: A humanist sans drawn over more than twenty years that embraces the irregularities of handmade letters, set as an honest, slightly imperfect counterpoint to geometric sans-serifs.
summary: A humanist sans drawn over two decades, embracing the warmth and quiet irregularity of handmade letters across eight weights with italics.
description: A humanist sans by Jonathan Hoefler, Tobias Frere-Jones and the H&Co studio, developed over more than twenty years. It rejects the rigidity of geometric sans-serifs in favour of an honest, slightly imperfect drawing where calligraphic memory survives in the curves and joins. Eight weights from Thin to Black with matching italics make it a flexible text and identity face for brand systems, magazines and interfaces that want sans clarity with a human voice.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co describe Ideal Sans as a typeface that celebrates the imperfections of handmade letters, an antidote to mechanical sans-serifs, drawn over more than two decades and released in eight weights with italics for editorial and brand use.

name: Idlewild
designer: Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: A geometric sans for display sizes inspired by mid-century lettering on travel posters, transit signs and airline graphics, drawn in three widths for headline use.
summary: A geometric display sans in three widths inspired by mid-century travel and transit lettering, made for headlines and signage.
description: A geometric display sans by Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co studio, drawing on the lettering of mid-century travel posters, airline graphics and transit systems. It comes in regular, wide and extra wide widths across five weights, giving it the airy poise of jet-age signage. Suited to headlines, packaging and identity work that wants the optimism of the period without slipping into pastiche.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co present Idlewild as a geometric sans designed for display, inspired by mid-century lettering from airline graphics, travel posters and transit systems, drawn in three widths and five weights for headline use.

name: Inkwell
designer: Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: A handwritten family that crosses categories, with serif, sans, script, blackletter and tuscan styles drawn in a unified informal hand for use across long documents and casual systems.
summary: A handwritten type system spanning sans, serif, script, blackletter and tuscan, drawn in a single informal voice for casual editorial and identity work.
description: A handwritten family by Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co studio that treats hand lettering as a system rather than a single style. Six subfamilies, including sans, serif, script, blackletter, tuscan and open, share the same casual hand, with multiple weights and italics. Useful for packaging, children's titles, casual editorial and identity work that wants a hand-drawn feel without the brittleness of one-note script fonts.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co describe Inkwell as a family of handwritten typefaces that bring together six subfamilies in a single informal voice, suited to long-form work where script fonts would tire and to casual identity systems.

name: Isotope
designer: Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: A square-shouldered display sans inspired by industrial and athletic lettering, drawn with squared curves and a technical edge for headlines, kits and packaging.
summary: A square-shouldered display sans with an industrial and athletic edge, built for headlines, sports identities and technical work.
description: A square sans by Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co studio, drawn with squared curves and tight terminals that recall stencilled industrial lettering, sports kit numbering and instrumentation. Five weights with italics give a compact display family for headlines, packaging and identity work where Knockout would feel too newspaper and Gotham too neutral.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co present Isotope as a square-shouldered sans inspired by industrial and athletic lettering, drawn for display use across five weights with italics, suited to sports, technology and packaging.

name: Knockout
classification_notes: A grotesque display family drawn on a nineteenth century model of independent widths and weights, with 32 styles plotted across a grid for headline systems.
summary: A 32-style grotesque display family by Jonathan Hoefler, drawn on a boxing-inspired grid of widths and weights for headline systems.
description: A grotesque display family by Jonathan Hoefler, set out on a nineteenth century model where widths and weights were drawn independently rather than interpolated. The result is 32 styles, named for boxing weight classes from Flyweight to Sumo, that map out a complete headline system for newspapers, sports, editorial and identity. Hoefler treats the family as a kit for setting expressive, hierarchy-rich pages.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co describe Knockout as a family of 32 sans-serifs drawn on a nineteenth century model, with widths and weights designed independently rather than interpolated, named after boxing classes and built for headline use.

name: Knox
designer: Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: A display family of inline and shadowed serifs inspired by nineteenth century show lettering, drawn for spirited signage and packaging.
summary: An inline and shadowed display serif drawing on nineteenth century show lettering for signage and packaging.
description: A display family by Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co studio, built around an inline serif and its shadow companion. It looks back to the chromatic show lettering of nineteenth century playbills and shopfronts, with high-contrast forms and detailed interiors. Suited to packaging, drinks branding and editorial titling where a sense of theatre is wanted.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co present Knox as a display family of inline and shadowed serifs inspired by nineteenth century show lettering, drawn for signage, packaging and titling where decorative chromatic effects are needed.

name: Landmark
designer: Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: A chromatic display family built from layered inline and shadow fonts, drawing on nineteenth century show lettering and travel graphics for posters and packaging.
summary: A chromatic inline display family built for layered colour work on posters, packaging and travel-inspired identities.
description: A chromatic display family by Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co studio, made up of layered inline and shadow fonts that can be stacked and coloured to recreate the look of nineteenth century show lettering and mid-century travel posters. Designed for large sizes in posters, packaging and identity work that wants depth and ornament.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co describe Landmark as a chromatic display family inspired by nineteenth century show lettering, made up of layered inline and shadow fonts that can be set in multiple colours for posters and packaging.

name: Leviathan
designer: Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: A compressed display sans inspired by Victorian poster lettering, drawn at extreme weight for headlines and packaging where space is tight and presence is large.
summary: A compressed display sans inspired by Victorian poster lettering, drawn heavy and narrow for headlines with maximum impact in minimum space.
description: A compressed display sans by Jonathan Hoefler and the H&Co studio that draws on the heavy poster lettering of Victorian Britain. Narrow, weighty and squared, it is built for headlines, mastheads and packaging where horizontal space is at a premium and impact is everything. Three weights map a small but versatile display kit.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co present Leviathan as a compressed display sans inspired by Victorian poster lettering, drawn heavy and narrow for headlines and packaging where space is tight and visual weight is needed.

name: Mercury Display
designer: Jonathan Hoefler, Tobias Frere-Jones, and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: The display cut of the Mercury system, drawn for large sizes with finer hairlines and sharper detail than the text family.
summary: The display cut of Mercury, drawn for headlines and editorial titling with finer hairlines and sharper detail than the text family.
description: A display serif by Jonathan Hoefler, Tobias Frere-Jones and the H&Co studio, drawn as the headline complement to Mercury Text. Sharper serifs, finer hairlines and tighter spacing make it suited to newspaper mastheads, magazine titles and editorial spreads where the text family would feel soft. Four weights with italics keep it usable for full headline hierarchies.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co describe Mercury Display as the display cut of the Mercury system, drawn with finer hairlines and sharper detail than the text family for use on mastheads, headlines and editorial titling.

name: Mercury Text
designer: Jonathan Hoefler, Tobias Frere-Jones, and the H&Co Design Studio
classification_notes: A newspaper and editorial text serif engineered for high-speed printing on uncoated stock, drawn in four grades to compensate for ink gain and press conditions.
summary: A newspaper text serif drawn in four grades to hold up across different inks, papers and press conditions in high-speed printing.
description: A newspaper and editorial text serif by Jonathan Hoefler, Tobias Frere-Jones and the H&Co studio. It was engineered with publishers in mind, drawn in four grades that anticipate ink gain on different papers and presses so the type holds its colour from broadsheet to glossy. Roman, bold and italics in each grade make it a complete editorial workhorse for newspapers, magazines and long-form digital text.
foundry_blurb: Hoefler & Co describe Mercury Text as a typeface engineered for the demands of high-speed newspaper printing, drawn in four grades to compensate for variations in ink, paper and press, and built for editorial workhorse use.

name: Nitro & Turbo
classification_notes: H&Co frame Nitro and Turbo as a sloped display pair built from a single extreme italic. Nitro is an aggressively sloped italic at heavy weight; Turbo is its backslanted counterpart. Both share a 28 degree slope, more than double the angle of most italics.
summary: A sloped display pair drawn for the New York Jets identity, pairing an aggressive 28 degree italic with a matching backslant.
description: A display family by Jonathan Hoefler, originally drawn for Pentagram's identity work on the New York Jets. Nitro is an aggressively sloped italic of massive weight, rooted in the heavy slanting lettering the team had used through its history. Turbo is the backslanted counterpart, a contra-italic that lets the family read correctly on both sides of a moving vehicle. Both share a 28 degree slope, so the family suits action graphics, sports identity, transport liveries and any setting where typography needs to imply force and motion.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Nitro as starting from the extreme, an aggressively sloped italic of massive weight, paired with an equal and opposite backslanted style they call Turbo. The family was designed for Pentagram's Michael Bierut as part of a new identity for the New York Jets, originally named Jets Bold, and is rooted in the heavy slanting forms the team had used through its fifty-year history. Both fonts share a fierce 28 degree slope, more than double the angle of most italics.

name: Numbers
classification_notes: H&Co describe Numbers as a collection of fifteen original numeral fonts, designed for Pentagram's 2006 calendar. Each font is built on a specific source of vernacular numbering, from playing cards and currency to railcars and street signs, rather than printing-type history.
summary: A set of fifteen numeral-only fonts drawn from playing cards, currency, railcars, subway signs and other vernacular sources.
description: A collection of fifteen numeral fonts by Jonathan Hoefler, designed for Pentagram's 2006 calendar and released by H&Co as a single family called Numbers. Each font draws on a specific vernacular source: Deuce from playing cards, Greenback from US currency, Depot from vintage railcars, Redbird from New York subway signs, Strasse from European enamel signs, Valuta from Hungarian banknotes, and so on through Bayside, Claimcheck, Delancey, Dividend, Indicia, Premium, Prospekt, Revenue and Trafalgar. The fonts revive a nineteenth-century practice where typefounders shipped numerals separately for tables, calendars and ledgers, and read as a kit of decorative tools for editorial, packaging and wayfinding rather than a working text family.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Numbers as a collection of fifteen original fonts created for Pentagram's 2006 calendar, reviving the nineteenth-century practice of providing numerals as separate fonts. They note that nineteenth-century specimen books often displayed sections containing fonts of numbers alone, with unique features for tables and calendars, and frame the Numbers series as drawing inspiration not from printing-type history but from modern environmental sources like playing cards, currency, railcars, subway signs, gas pumps and enamel signs.

name: Obsidian
designer: Hoefler & Co (Andy Clymer, Jonathan Hoefler, Sara Soskolne)
classification_notes: H&Co position Obsidian as a decorated display face that uses digital means to achieve traditional ends. It treats Surveyor's letterforms as three-dimensional objects, with proprietary tools that apply virtual light sources at varying position, angle and intensity to produce engraved-style highlights.
summary: A decorated display face that treats letterforms as three-dimensional objects, lit by virtual light sources to imitate engraved banknote and diploma lettering.
description: A decorated display family by H&Co that uses custom software to apply virtual highlights to letterforms, producing the engraved, dimensional look familiar from banknotes, diplomas and ornamental nineteenth-century printing. Built on the foundation of Surveyor, Obsidian spans roman and italic with around 1,400 glyphs, smart swashes that automatically improve text rhythm, and OpenType stylistic sets that swap in alternate looks. The result is a single-weight display face for packaging, certificates, luxury identity and editorial titling where you want overt ornament rather than a working text system.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Obsidian as a majestic typeface that uses digital means to achieve traditional ends, paying homage to nineteenth-century steelplate engraving while remaining relevant to contemporary designers. The family is built on the foundation of Surveyor, with proprietary tools developed in-house to interpret two-dimensional letterforms as three-dimensional objects through virtual light sources that vary in position, angle and intensity. Obsidian spans roman and italic styles, around 1,400 glyphs, smart swashes and OpenType stylistic sets.

name: Operator
designer: Andy Clymer, Jonathan Hoefler, Sara Soskolne
classification_notes: H&Co describe Operator as a non-typewriter typewriter face. The primary Operator family has natural proportional widths; Operator Mono is the fixed-width companion intended for code editors and other monospaced settings.
summary: A typewriter-inspired family in both proportional and monospaced widths, drawn for editorial voice and for code editors.
description: A slab-serif family by Andy Clymer, Jonathan Hoefler and Sara Soskolne that takes the voice of typewriting and rebuilds it as a full typographic system. The primary Operator family is proportional, allowing letters to take their natural widths, while Operator Mono is a fixed-width companion for programming environments and any setting that needs a true monospace. Both run across multiple weights with matching italics, and a ScreenSmart cut is provided for small-size screen work. Reach for it on editorial features, charitable appeals and corporate communications that want the earnestness of a typewritten letter, and on code editors that want a typewriter feel with real weight range.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Operator as a typeface rooted in the traditions of typewriting, designed to offer a new voice for designers and a new tool for developers. They note that typesetting is formal, handwriting is informal and typewriting sits between the two, but that actual typewriter faces lack the weight range, small caps and italic distinctiveness designers need, and their fixed-width construction disrupts the rhythm of text. Operator builds on the aesthetic of typewriting but sheds its mechanical constraints, with a proportional primary family and a fixed-width Operator Mono drawn for programming environments.

name: Peristyle
classification_notes: H&Co frame Peristyle as a high-contrast condensed sans, designed to restore the effortless chic of the high-contrast sans without leaning into Art Deco, sixties funk or futurist styling. A Peristyle Stencil cut is included.
summary: A high-contrast condensed sans for fashion and editorial work, designed to dodge period flavour and read as drama rather than nostalgia.
description: A condensed sans-serif family by H&Co built around amplified stroke contrast, drawn to bring drama and elegance to fashion, editorial and luxury work without tipping into Art Deco or sixties revival. Peristyle runs across multiple weights with italics, and the family extends into Peristyle Stencil, including bicolor Layers fonts whose colours balance automatically. OpenType stylistic sets let designers swap in alternate looks for headlines, mastheads and packaging.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Peristyle as a family of stylish condensed typefaces designed to restore the effortless chic of the high-contrast sans. They note that amplifying an alphabet's contrast turns up the drama and has long been used by novelty typefaces, but that Peristyle works to avoid looking forward or backward, summoning the energy of contrasting darkness and light to make a typeface for today. The family dodges eccentricity to find drama, elegance and style, and includes a Peristyle Stencil cut with colour-balancing Layers fonts.

name: Quarto
classification_notes: H&Co describe Quarto as a modern adaptation of Hendrik van den Keere's Two-Line Double Pica Roman of 1570 to 1573, intended for use at sixteen point and above. They position it as a fiery display face in the Dutch taste, drawn for controlled contrast.
summary: A Dutch Baroque display serif after Van den Keere, drawn for headlines that want dark colour, crisp serifs and controlled contrast.
description: A display serif by Jonathan Hoefler based on Hendrik van den Keere's Two-Line Double Pica Roman of 1570 to 1573, designed for use at sixteen point and above. Quarto runs across multiple weights with italics, with consistently dark vertical strokes offset by bright, crisp serifs and moments of lavish roundness. Its large x-height, short descenders and trim serifs invite tight tracking and solid leading, and the italics balance fluid motion against a steadfast rhythm. Reach for it on editorial mastheads, book jackets and headline systems that want a Baroque voice without slipping into pastiche.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Quarto as a modern adaptation of the Van den Keere style, designed for use at sixteen point and above. They cite Hendrik van den Keere's Two-Line Double Pica Roman of 1570 to 1573 as the foundation for the family, an arresting design marked by striking dramatic tensions, and frame Quarto as exploring controlled contrast through consistently dark strokes, bright serifs, resolute verticality and lavish roundness, with italics drawn for fluid motion checked by steady rhythm.

name: Requiem
classification_notes: H&Co describe Requiem as inspired by an illustration in Ludovico Vicentino degli Arrighi's writing manual Il Modo de Temperare le Penne (1523), with a chancery-cursive italic and three optical sizes per style.
summary: A Renaissance humanist serif after Arrighi's 1523 writing manual, with chancery italics, three optical sizes and a full set of ornaments.
description: A Renaissance serif by Jonathan Hoefler, drawn from an illustration in Ludovico Vicentino degli Arrighi's 1523 writing manual Il Modo de Temperare le Penne. The display roman and small caps take their structure from Arrighi's elegant rendering of the classical alphabet, the lowercase is an invention in the same spirit, and the italic returns to the full-blooded chancery cursive for which Arrighi is most famous. Each style is provided in three optical sizes (Fine, Text and Display) to preserve delicate features at every scale, and Requiem Ornaments includes two decorative cartouche alphabets and a set of printers' flowers. Italic ligature sets and decorative alternates make it suited to book typography, editorial features and identity work in a humanist register.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Requiem as inspired by an illustration in a sixteenth-century writing manual, celebrating the fertile world of Renaissance humanism. They trace its display roman and small caps to Arrighi's Il Modo de Temperare le Penne of 1523, and its italic to his chancery cursive. Requiem is provided in three optical sizes per style to tackle different size ranges, and includes Requiem Ornaments with two decorative cartouche alphabets and printers' flowers, plus italic ligature sets and decorative alternates across romans, italics and small caps.

name: Ringside
classification_notes: H&Co describe Ringside as a sans-serif system designed for diversity rather than homogeneity, picking up from Knockout but built for text, with six widths that take different design decisions instead of uniformly stretching one master.
summary: An American grotesque superfamily built for text, with six widths that each take different design decisions instead of stretching one master.
description: A sans-serif superfamily by H&Co that picks up from Knockout but is drawn for text rather than headlines. Ringside spans six widths (Compressed, Condensed, Narrow, Regular, Wide and Extra Wide), each with a companion italic and a range of weights, and adds a ScreenSmart cut for small-size screen work. Each width takes its own design decisions: the Compressed and Condensed pair flat-sided romans with swelling italics, the Narrow and Regular favour rounder forms, and the Wide and Extra Wide introduce fully curved characters with diagonal stroke endings. Tabular figures, fractions and an extended character set make it serviceable for editorial systems, identity work and information design that needs typographic variety inside one family.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Ringside as a sans serif shaped by new challenges, new influences and new ideas, picking up where Knockout left off but designed for text rather than headlines. They note that its proportions, fit and details are designed to thrive at the smallest sizes, that every weight and width has a companion italic, and that six parallel families of ScreenSmart webfonts ensure flawless screen performance. The family follows different design decisions in each part, so Compressed and Condensed pair flat-sided romans with swelling italics, Narrow and Regular use rounder forms, and Wide and Extra Wide introduce fully curved characters with diagonal stroke endings.

name: Saracen
classification_notes: H&Co describe Saracen as the Latin (wedge serif) member of The Proteus Project, a collection of four interchangeable nineteenth-century display families that share character widths so they can be swapped almost invisibly.
summary: A Latin wedge-serif display face in The Proteus Project, drawn as a missing Victorian gem with widths that match its three sibling families.
description: A display serif by Jonathan Hoefler that fills the Latin (wedge serif) slot in The Proteus Project, a four-family system whose members share character widths so they can be interchanged almost invisibly. H&Co note that although the wedge serif is a nineteenth-century innovation, Saracen does not resemble any font of the era, and is offered as the straightforward proportion that Victorian typefounders somehow never produced. Useful for posters, packaging, signage and identity work that wants Victorian wedge-serif character with a clean, contemporary build, and for editorial settings that want to swap between Latin, Egyptian, Tuscan and grotesque styles without disturbing line breaks.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Saracen as a missing Victorian gem, the Latin (wedge serif) member of The Proteus Project, a collection of four interchangeable type families designed in different nineteenth-century styles. They note that although the wedge serif printing type is a nineteenth-century innovation, Saracen does not resemble any font from this era, and find it mysterious that typefounders of the Victorian age who explored extremes never made a Latin font of this straightforward proportion. Its transposability with the other Proteus Project families is a defining feature.

name: Sentinel
classification_notes: H&Co describe Sentinel as a fresh take on the classical slab serif, addressing the shortcomings of Geometrics, Antiques and Clarendons by providing italics across the full range, a working text family and a consistent progression of weights.
summary: A Clarendon-style slab serif drawn to fix the historical gaps, with italics across six weights and an optical cut for text and screen.
description: A slab-serif family by Jonathan Hoefler and Tobias Frere-Jones that reworks the Clarendon tradition into a complete, modern typographic system. Sentinel borrows the contrasting thicks and thins of the Antique style but trades its frumpier mannerisms for contemporary detail, and unlike most Clarendons it carries italics across the full range. Six weights run from Light to Black, with short-ranging figures and language coverage tuned for text, and a Sentinel ScreenSmart cut handles small sizes on screen. Reach for it on editorial features, identity work, packaging and information design that wants the warmth of a slab serif with the discipline of a modern text family.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Sentinel as the slab serif that works, drawn for everyone who has ever wished Clarendons had italics, whose favourite slab serif is shy a few weights, or who has needed a slab serif to thrive in text. They frame the design as a response to the crippling deficiencies of the classical slab serif: Geometrics that cannot extend to heavier weights without compromise, Clarendons that lack lighter weights and italics, and Antiques whose mannerisms feel dated. Sentinel offers a complete range of styles, six weights from Light to Black, italics throughout and a ScreenSmart cut for screen rendering.

name: Surveyor
classification_notes: H&Co describe Surveyor as a celebration of nineteenth-century engraved map lettering, with compact proportions, delicate serifs and a sinewy italic. Each style is offered in three optical sizes (Fine, Text and Display) to handle a wide range of sizes.
summary: A serif system inspired by engraved map lettering, offered in three optical sizes with smart swashes and small caps across the family.
description: A serif family by H&Co built on the engraved lettering of nineteenth-century maps, originally drawn for Martha Stewart Living and later expanded into a full system. Surveyor runs across multiple weights, with each style available in three optical sizes (Fine for the smallest cover work, Text for body copy and Display for headlines), and a ScreenSmart cut for screen rendering. Roman and italic small caps, tabular figures, fractions and an OpenType-driven smart swash feature support recipes, calendars, step-by-step layouts, maps and editorial features that want compact proportions, delicate serifs and a sinewy italic.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Surveyor as a Mapmaker's Letter, a monumental family of typefaces inspired by the engraved lettering of nineteenth-century cartography. They cite copperplate engraving and cartographic needs as the source of its compact proportions, delicate serifs and sinewy italic, and trace the family back to a 2000 commission from Martha Stewart Living. Surveyor is now offered in five weights from Light to Black, with three optical sizes per style (Fine, Text and Display), roman and italic small caps, tabular figures, fractions, smart swashes and a Surveyor ScreenSmart cut for screen rendering.

name: The Proteus Project
classification_notes: H&Co frame Proteus as a coordinated system of four ultrabold display families: Acropolis (engraved Greek), Saracen (slab Western), Leviathan (sans grotesque) and Zigguarat (Egyptian slab), all sharing the same widths and weights so they can be interchanged in the same setting.
summary: A four-family display system from Jonathan Hoefler that ties Acropolis, Saracen, Leviathan and Ziggurat to the same widths and weights for big nineteenth-century headlines.
description: A coordinated display system by Jonathan Hoefler that revives the heavy nineteenth-century commercial styles of broadsides and wood type. Four families, Acropolis, Saracen, Leviathan and Ziggurat, share the same proportions so designers can swap an engraved Greek for an Egyptian slab inside the same composition without retuning. Built for posters, packaging and editorial mastheads where the typography is the picture.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe The Proteus Project as a single design exercise that produced four interrelated display families, each rooted in a different nineteenth-century tradition but built on shared widths and weights so they can be combined freely.

name: Topaz
classification_notes: H&Co classify Topaz as a reverse-contrast (Italian) display face, drawing on nineteenth-century circus and showbill lettering where horizontal strokes are heavier than verticals.
summary: A reverse-contrast display from Hoefler & Co that revives nineteenth-century Italian and showbill lettering for posters and packaging.
description: A reverse-contrast display by Jonathan Hoefler that pulls weight onto the horizontals in the manner of nineteenth-century Italian and showbill type. Built for posters, mastheads and packaging where you want the eye-catching strangeness of a circus broadside without leaning on a literal pastiche. Single weight, intended to be set large.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Topaz as a reverse-contrast display face in the tradition of nineteenth-century Italian types, with heavier horizontals than verticals, drawing on the lettering of showbills and posters from that period.

name: Tungsten
classification_notes: H&Co classify Tungsten as a compressed sans serif, narrow but not pinched, drawing on the friendly authority of municipal and athletic lettering rather than the brittle geometry of older condensed gothics.
summary: A compressed sans from Hoefler & Co that fits big voices into small horizontal space, leaning on athletic and civic lettering rather than brittle condensed gothic.
description: A compressed sans by Jonathan Hoefler and Berton Hasebe that pitches itself between the mechanical condensed gothic and the more humane lettering on stadium fascias and municipal signs. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, plus narrower and rounded subfamilies. Reach for it on sports identities, political campaigns and editorial headlines that need to shout in tight measures without becoming hostile.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Tungsten as a compressed sans serif designed to be narrow without feeling pinched, citing the friendly authority of athletic uniforms and municipal lettering rather than the harshness of older condensed gothics.

name: Tungsten Rounded
classification_notes: H&Co frame Tungsten Rounded as the softened cut of Tungsten, replacing the family's straight terminals with rounded ones for a warmer, more inviting voice while keeping the same metrics.
summary: The rounded cut of Tungsten, swapping straight terminals for soft ones to keep the same compressed authority with a warmer voice.
description: A rounded compressed sans by Jonathan Hoefler and Berton Hasebe that softens Tungsten's terminals without changing its metrics. Seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, designed to sit alongside the parent family. Used where a campaign or sports identity wants Tungsten's tight horizontal economy with a friendlier finish, particularly on packaging and signage.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Tungsten Rounded as the softened companion to Tungsten, sharing the same compressed proportions and weight range but replacing the straight terminals with rounded ones for a warmer reading.

name: Verlag
classification_notes: H&Co classify Verlag as a geometric sans rooted in the lettering of the Guggenheim Museum, drawing on 1930s American modernism rather than the German Bauhaus geometric tradition.
summary: A geometric sans from Hoefler & Co commissioned by the Guggenheim, drawn from 1930s American modernism rather than European Bauhaus geometry.
description: A geometric sans by Jonathan Hoefler and Tobias Frere-Jones, commissioned by the Guggenheim Museum and rooted in the lettering Frank Lloyd Wright applied to the building's facade. Five weights from Light to Black with matching italics, plus Compressed and Condensed widths for tighter measures. Sits in cultural institutions, civic identity and editorial systems that want geometric clarity with an American rather than European accent.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Verlag as a geometric sans serif commissioned by the Guggenheim Museum, drawing on the lettering of the building itself and on 1930s American modernism, distinct from the German Bauhaus tradition that shaped most other geometric sans serifs.

name: Vitesse
classification_notes: H&Co classify Vitesse as a geometric slab serif, built on a square skeleton with rounded corners, evoking the lettering of motorsport, dashboards and industrial signage rather than the literary slab tradition.
summary: A geometric slab from Hoefler & Co built on a softened square skeleton, drawing on motorsport and industrial lettering rather than the literary slab tradition.
description: A geometric slab serif by Jonathan Hoefler and Tobias Frere-Jones, drawn around a square armature with gently rounded corners. Six weights from Thin to Black with matching italics. Reaches for the visual world of dashboards, race liveries and industrial signage, which makes it useful for sports, automotive and technical brands wanting a slab that feels engineered rather than bookish.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Vitesse as a geometric slab serif built on a square skeleton with rounded corners, drawing on the lettering of motorsport, dashboards and industrial signage rather than the literary slab tradition.

name: Whitney
classification_notes: H&Co classify Whitney as a humanist sans designed to bridge editorial and signage use, drawn for the Whitney Museum to perform both as wayfinding and as continuous text.
summary: A humanist sans from Tobias Frere-Jones drawn for the Whitney Museum to handle both wayfinding and editorial setting in one family.
description: A humanist sans by Tobias Frere-Jones, originally drawn for the Whitney Museum to handle wayfinding and editorial running text inside the same identity. Six weights with matching italics, plus Narrow, Condensed and ScreenSmart cuts. Ends up in a lot of corporate, institutional and editorial systems that want a sans which is calm at text size but still legible on a sign.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Whitney as a humanist sans serif drawn for the Whitney Museum, designed to perform two jobs at once: as a signage face for wayfinding and as a comfortable text face for catalogues and publications.

name: Whitney Condensed
classification_notes: H&Co frame Whitney Condensed as the most compact width within the Whitney system, holding the family's humanist voice at tighter measures for headlines and tabular settings.
summary: The condensed cut of Whitney, holding the family's humanist signage voice at tighter measures for headlines, charts and forms.
description: A condensed humanist sans by Tobias Frere-Jones that compresses Whitney without losing its signage clarity. Six weights from Light to Black with matching italics, designed to sit alongside the standard and Narrow widths. Used in editorial, financial and information design where space is short but the brand still wants Whitney's calm institutional reading.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Whitney Condensed as the most compact width within the Whitney system, retaining the family's humanist proportions and signage clarity while saving horizontal space for headlines, tables and forms.

name: Whitney Narrow
classification_notes: H&Co frame Whitney Narrow as an intermediate width sitting between the standard Whitney and Whitney Condensed, designed to save space without the visible compression of the Condensed cut.
summary: The narrow cut of Whitney, saving space without looking compressed for editorial, signage and information design.
description: A narrow humanist sans by Tobias Frere-Jones that sits between Whitney and Whitney Condensed in the family system. Six weights from Light to Black with matching italics. Used where designers want to fit more words on a line or a sign without slipping into a visibly condensed voice, particularly in editorial mastheads, museum labels and financial documents.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Whitney Narrow as an intermediate width within the Whitney system, designed to save horizontal space without the obvious compression of Whitney Condensed, keeping the family's humanist signage voice intact.

name: Ziggurat
classification_notes: H&Co classify Ziggurat as a heavy Egyptian slab in the nineteenth-century broadside tradition, part of The Proteus Project alongside Acropolis, Saracen and Leviathan, sharing their widths and weights.
summary: A heavy Egyptian slab from Jonathan Hoefler, part of the Proteus Project and rooted in nineteenth-century broadside lettering.
description: An Egyptian slab serif by Jonathan Hoefler, drawn from the heavy slab traditions of nineteenth-century broadsides and wood type. Five weights from Light to Black, with the heaviest cuts doing most of the work. Released as part of The Proteus Project, sharing widths and weights with Acropolis, Saracen and Leviathan so the four families can be combined in the same setting. Built for posters, packaging and editorial mastheads.
foundry_blurb: H&Co describe Ziggurat as a heavy Egyptian slab serif drawn in the tradition of nineteenth-century broadside and wood type, released as one of the four families of The Proteus Project alongside Acropolis, Saracen and Leviathan, all sharing the same widths and weights.
